One of the most remarkable studies ever done in the social sciences looked at the impact of the principle of commitment and consistency. Two Stanford University professors had researchers go door-to-door in Palo Alto, California, the wealthiest part of Silicon Valley. As part of their study, they told homeowners that they were from the Committee for Traffic Safety. Then they made a truly outrageous request. They said that because the safety of their community streets was so important, they wanted permission to post an enormous, ugly, handwritten billboard on the homeowners' lawn that read, Drive Carefully. The researchers even offered to dig the post holes themselves for the homeowners. This billboard was so large that it would have obscured the view of the residents' home from the street by 30 percent. Of course, most of the homeowners said no to such a preposterous request. In fact, only 17 percent of them said yes. In the second experimental condition, however, researchers went door-to-door to the same neighborhood but to different houses. For these homeowners, the researchers asked if they would display a small 3 by 5 inch card reading Drive Carefully in their front windows. Almost 100 percent agreed it was such a minor request. But that wasn't the end of the experiment for these residents. Two weeks later, different researchers revisited the same households that had agreed to display the 3 by 5 inch card and made a much larger request. The one that was to put that ugly, large Drive Carefully billboard on their lawns. Recall that only 17 percent of the homeowners in the other group had agreed to this when it was the only one requested. But these people in the second condition had first agreed to display that small card in their windows for two weeks, advocating driver safety. How many of them do you think now agreed to put that ugly Drive Carefully billboard on their lawns? It was an amazing 76 percent of these homeowners in the second experimental condition who agreed to that large request. Why? Why was there an increase of 400 percent in the willingness to put up that large sign? The only difference between the first request to install the large ugly billboard and the second request was what came first. In the second condition, the homeowners first agreed to a small request to put the 3 by 5 inch card in their windows for two weeks prior to receiving this much larger request. The second request to post this billboard was for the same driver safety cause. But the Palo Alto homeowners who first displayed the 3 by 5 inch card in their front windows wanted to remain consistent with their prior stand, even though it involved a small commitment that clearly had a big, big effect. So we are internally motivated to remain consistent with our prior choices. This is why you must be prepared to relate your request to prior commitments that your audience has already made. These initial commitments can take the form of prior words or positions or actions.
